title: How to manage a venous occlusion
filename: How to manage a venous occlusion.en.vtt
excerpt: Probably that the greater surface area there enables them to enables the hyaluronidase to permeate into those vessels and unoccluded effectively so if you did cause venous occlusion how would you manage it well it makes sense that you could manage it like you would a vascular ill…

segments.text: probably that the greater surface area there enables them to enables the hyaluronidase to permeate into those vessels and unoccluded effectively so if you did cause venous occlusion how would you manage it well it makes sense that you could manage it like you would a vascular illusion which is that we do this process of warm compress massage hyaluronidase and repeat that until blood flow is normal now the big difference with that with the venous occlusion is if you're going to be massaging it i think it's going to be highly effective if you massage a vascular occlusion that's in an arterial you're effectively massaging a lot of the filler into the arterioles and then into the capillaries which is fine because probably that the greater surface area there enables them to enable the hyaluronidase to permeate into those vessels and unoccluded effectively the thing with a venous occlusion is it's gonna be much easier to move it away from the site so we will be effectively causing those tiny micro emboli going into the lungs a small pulmonary embolism but

pulmonary embolism but hopefully so small that you would get zero side effects from it now think about that that's quite important mostly with a pulmonary embolism we're talking very big blood clots that cause clinical symptoms so 0.25 ml of dermal filler is probably not going to cause a problem now i have no evidence for that because it's never been researched it's very hard to prove um but it does make sense to me that it's very likely to cause any problems so massage could be good but then you're saying you would also do the usual with the holidays yeah i think that the normal process is just going to seem to work a lot quicker um i wouldn't recommend only massaging if you think it's impeding blood flow if you've got a significant delay refill because obviously the other thing is it's very hard to tell unless you're a sonographer how are you going to tell whether it's in a vein or an artery all you've got is delayed refill and so it makes sense to treat it as you would any vascular occlusion i just think working from first principles it may be easier to solve because you've got these veins that you can easily massage the product out of
